29: Josiah’s death at Megiddo — wayyēlek liqrāʾtô wayyᵉmîtēhû bᵉmegiddô kᵉrōʾtô, ‘he went to meet him and Pharaoh Neco killed him at Megiddo when he saw him’ — occurs in the context of the geopolitical transition between Assyrian and Babylonian hegemony. The year 609 BCE was the critical transition year: Assyria was collapsing under Babylonian pressure; Egypt under Pharaoh Neco II was marching north to support the remnant Assyrian forces and establish Egyptian control over the Levantine corridor before Babylon could fill the vacuum. Josiah’s interception of Neco’s march at Megiddo — the classic strategic chokepoint controlling the road from Egypt through the Jezreel Valley — is his attempt to prevent a hostile power from crossing Israelite territory. The strategic reasoning was sound; the outcome was fatal. Megiddo’s historical identity as the decisive battle-site is embedded in the name: Revelation 16:16 uses Har Megiddon (Mount Megiddo) as the site of the final eschatological battle — Armageddon — drawing on Megiddo’s accumulated symbolic weight as the place where decisive battles are fought and kings die. The Chronicler (2 Chronicles 35:20-24) provides additional detail, including Neco’s claim that his march was at God’s command — an oracle Josiah did not heed, adding to the theological complexity of the narrative.
1: Josiah assembles the elders of Judah and Jerusalem for a covenant ceremony at the temple. The formal assembly (wayyiqbōṣ) of elders, priests, prophets, and all the people — small and great — reflects the Deuteronomic renewal pattern: the whole covenant community participates in the reading and ratification of the covenant. The formal assembly echoes Joshua’s Shechem covenant renewal (Josh 24) and foreshadows Ezra’s public Torah reading (Nehemiah 8:1–12).
2: Josiah reads aloud in their hearing all the words of the book of the covenant found in the temple. The identification of this book with Deuteronomy — or a core of it — has been the dominant hypothesis since de Wette (1805). The public reading of the covenant document fulfills the Deuteronomic prescription for a septennial reading at the Feast of Tabernacles (Deuteronomy 31:10–13) and parallels the inauguration reading by Joshua (Joshua 8:34–35).
3: The king stands by the pillar (ha’ammûḏ) and makes a covenant before YHWH to follow with all his heart and soul. The pillar designation echoes the royal pillar of Joash’s coronation (2 Kings 11:14), marking it as the official royal standing place for covenant ceremonies. The tripartite formula — all heart, soul, and might — is Deuteronomic (Deuteronomy 6:5) and appears verbatim in the evaluation of Josiah’s devotion (v25). All the people join the covenant.
4: Josiah commands the high priest Hilkiah to remove all vessels made for Baal, Asherah, and all the host of heaven from the temple. The three-part cult catalogue — Baal, Asherah, the host of heaven — represents three distinct streams of apostate worship: Canaanite storm-god cult, Canaanite fertility religion, and the Assyrian-influenced astral religion. The temple had become a multi-cult shrine under Manasseh and Amon; the reform’s first act is the systematic removal of its entire alien apparatus.
5: Josiah deposes the kᵉmārîm — the idolatrous priests (qᵉmārîm in Zephaniah 1:4; Hosea 10:5) — whom the kings of Judah had appointed to make offerings at the high places and to the sun, moon, and constellations. The term kᵉmārîm is an Aramaic loanword (cf. Syriac kāmrāʾ) used exclusively in pejorative contexts for priests of non-YHWH cults. The list of astral objects — sun, moon, mazālôt (the zodiacal signs or planets) — reflects the Mesopotamian astral religion that had penetrated Judahite temple practice under Assyrian influence.
6: The Asherah is brought out of the temple, burned at the Kidron Valley, beaten to dust, and scattered on graves. The Kidron Valley served as the primary receptacle for defiling matter from Jerusalem’s cultic purges (cf. 2 Kings 15:13; 2 Chronicles 29:16). The sequence — removal, burning, grinding, scattering on graves — represents progressively more thorough defilement: the object is first destroyed then made ritually impure by contact with the dead. Asherah cult objects (wooden poles or stylized trees) are attested at Kuntillet Ajrud and Khirbet el-Qom inscriptions of the 8th century.
7: Josiah tears down the houses of the male cult prostitutes (qᵉḏēšîm) at the temple entrance where women wove garments for the Asherah. The qᵉḏēšîm (literally ‘consecrated ones’) have been variously identified as cultic functionaries in the fertility cult, though modern scholarship debates whether the term always denotes sexual service. The weaving of garments for the Asherah is paralleled in Mesopotamian texts describing the dressing of divine statues; it represents maintenance of the goddess’s cult image.
8: Josiah brings all the priests from the cities of Judah to Jerusalem and defiles the high places where they had burned offerings, from Geba to Beersheba. The geographical sweep — Geba (northern border of Judah) to Beersheba (southern border) — is the standard formula for all of Judah (cf. 1 Samuel 3:20 for ‘from Dan to Beersheba’). The centralization of the priesthood at Jerusalem is the implementing act of Deuteronomy’s prescription that sacrifice be offered only at the chosen place.
9: Despite the removal of high-place priests to Jerusalem, they do not serve at the Jerusalem altar but eat unleavened bread among their brothers. The distinction between high-place priests and temple priests represents a practical compromise between the reform’s ideological goal (centralized worship) and its economic/social reality (the temple priesthood’s resistance to admitting country clergy). The category of priests who eat temple support without altar service may correspond to the Deuteronomic Levites dependent on community support (Deuteronomy 18:6–8).
10: Josiah defiles Topheth in the Ben-Hinnom Valley so that no one can sacrifice a son or daughter by fire to Molech. The Topheth — whose etymology may connect to the Aramaic term for fireplace or the Hebrew tōp (drum, used to drown out cries) — was the cultic precinct for child sacrifice. Archaeological evidence from Carthage and Phoenician colonies documents molk-sacrifice (burned child offerings) consistent with the biblical Molech cult. Jeremiah attests that this practice continued after Josiah’s reform (Jeremiah 7:31; 2 Kings 32:35).
11: Josiah removes the horses that the kings of Judah had dedicated to the sun, at the entrance to the temple near the chamber of the officer Nathan-melech, and burns the chariots of the sun with fire. Solar chariot symbolism is well-attested in Mesopotamian and Syro-Palestinian iconography; the winged solar disk was a common royal emblem. The installation of horses — perhaps statues or live animals — at the temple entrance represents the incorporation of solar cult iconography into official royal religion, consistent with Assyrian vassal religious practices.
12: Josiah tears down the altars on the roof of the upper chamber of Ahaz and the altars that Manasseh had made in the two courts of the temple. Rooftop altars (cf. Jeremiah 19:13; 2 Kings 32:29; Zephaniah 1:5) were used for astral worship because the open sky provided unobstructed access to celestial objects. Ahaz had introduced these during the period of Assyrian domination (2 Kings 16:10–16); Manasseh expanded them. The destruction of multi-generational installations underscores how deeply the reform had to dig.
13: Josiah defiles the high places east of Jerusalem that Solomon built — for Ashtoreth of the Sidonians, Chemosh of Moab, and Milcom of the Ammonites. These sites, standing for three centuries since Solomon’s apostasy (1 Kings 11:7), are on the Mount of Offense (lit. ‘Mount of Destruction,’ a polemical reshaping of ‘Mount of Anointing’). The durability of these cult sites — surviving from Solomon to Josiah — illustrates the persistence of royal patronage of foreign cults as diplomatic and personal accommodation.
14: Josiah smashes the sacred pillars (maṣṣēḇôt), cuts down the Asherah poles, and fills the sites with human bones. The use of human bones to defile a cultic site renders it permanently impure under Levitical law (Numbers 19:16); bones from opened tombs were strategically spread to consecrate the destruction. The maṣṣēḇôt (standing stones) had been prohibited by Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 16:22) but were widely used throughout the ANE as memorial stones, boundary markers, and cult objects.
15: The altar at Bethel — erected by Jeroboam ben Nebat who made Israel sin — is torn down, its high place burned, and the Asherah burned. The fall of Bethel’s altar to a Judahite king is remarkable: Bethel was formally within the former northern kingdom’s territory, now under Assyrian provincial administration. Josiah’s ability to act at Bethel reflects either Assyrian weakness during the decline of Ashur-uballit II (612–609 BCE) or the effective extension of Judahite authority into the Megiddo region during the Assyrian withdrawal.
16: Josiah turns and sees the tombs on the mountain and has the bones taken from them to burn on the altar, defiling it according to the word of YHWH proclaimed by the man of God from Judah (1 Kings 13:2). The fulfillment notice — over three centuries after the unnamed prophet’s oracle at Jeroboam’s dedication of the Bethel altar — is one of the most extended prophetic fulfillments in Kings. The precision of the fulfillment — burning bones on the altar, the man of God from Judah — validates the deuteronomistic framework of prophecy and fulfillment.
17: Josiah asks what monument he sees; the men of the city explain it is the grave of the man of God who came from Judah and proclaimed these things against the altar. The identification of the grave and its preservation — by locals who have maintained the tradition for three centuries — reflects the ANE custom of preserving the tombs of holy men. The narrative detail authenticates the prophetic tradition while setting up the exemption that follows.
18: Josiah orders the bones left undisturbed — sparing the man of God’s bones and those of the prophet who came from Samaria. The second prophet buried alongside is the Bethelite prophet of 1 Kings 13:11–31 who had deceived the man of God but ultimately acknowledged his authentic prophetic status. The preservation of both men’s bones is an act of respect for prophetic office, even where the prophets had been in conflict.
19: Josiah removes all the shrines of the high places in the cities of Samaria that the kings of Israel had made. The extension of Josiah’s reform into the territory of the former northern kingdom reflects the political vacuum created by the Assyrian collapse: Ashurbanipal died around 627 BCE, and the following decades saw rapid Assyrian contraction, allowing Josiah to expand Judahite political and religious influence northward.
20: Josiah kills all the priests of the high places on the altars and burns human bones on them, then returns to Jerusalem. The killing of the northern priests is the most violent act of the reform — comparable to Elijah’s massacre of the Baal prophets (1 Kings 18:40). The reform’s violence against clergy who were actively maintaining forbidden cult reflects Deuteronomy’s capital prescription for those who lead Israel into apostasy (Deuteronomy 13:5–9; 2 Kings 17:12).
21: Josiah commands all the people to keep the Passover as written in this book of the covenant. The identification of the discovered document as containing Passover legislation connects it with the Deuteronomic Passover regulations (Deuteronomy 16:1–8), which differ from the Exodus Passover in prescribing celebration at the central sanctuary rather than in individual homes. The Passover as a national pilgrimage festival at Jerusalem marks the climax of centralization.
22: The editorial notice that no such Passover had been kept since the days of the judges — not during all the days of the kings of Israel or of Judah — is a deuteronomistic hyperbole asserting the unprecedented scale of Josiah’s celebration. Hezekiah had also held a major Passover (2 Chronicles 30:1–27), but Kings either downplays this or uses a different evaluative criterion. The reference to the judges period suggests the Passover had degenerated from its Mosaic form even before the monarchy.
23: The Passover is held in Jerusalem in the eighteenth year of King Josiah — 622 BCE by conventional dating. The specific dating — the same year as the book’s discovery — ties the Passover celebration directly to the reform’s climax. The eighteenth year of Josiah (640–609 BCE) = 622 BCE, which aligns with the period of Assyrian weakness following Ashurbanipal’s effective withdrawal from active western campaigning.
24: Josiah eliminates the mediums, spiritists, household gods (tᵉrāpîm), idols (gillûlîm), and all the detestable things seen in Judah and Jerusalem. The tᵉrāpîm (household gods) were small figurines used in domestic cult and divination; Rachel’s theft of Laban’s tᵉrāpîm (Genesis 31:34) and Michal’s use of one as a decoy (1 Samuel 19:13–16) show their persistence. The term gillûlîm (‘dung-pellets’) is Ezekiel’s characteristic pejorative for idols.
25: The deuteronomistic eulogy of Josiah: no king before him turned to YHWH with all his heart, all his soul, all his might, according to all the Torah of Moses. The triple Deuteronomic formula (bᵉkhol lᵉḇāḇô ûḇᵉkhol napšô ûḇᵉkhol mᵉʾōḏô) from Deuteronomy 6:5 is applied as a royal standard for the first and only time to any king in Kings. The superlative evaluation — none before him — echoes the structure of other Deuteronomic superlatives (Deuteronomy 34:10 for Moses) and sets Josiah as the high point of the monarchy.
26: Nevertheless, YHWH does not turn from his fierce anger against Judah because of all Manasseh’s provocations. The theological counterweight to Josiah’s incomparable devotion is the inherited guilt of Manasseh’s fifty-five-year reign. The Deuteronomistic Historian presents a stark theological paradox: the best reform effort produces no national reprieve because the covenant damage from Manasseh’s apostasy is irreversible within the monarchical framework.
27: YHWH declares his intention to remove Judah from his presence as he removed Israel, and to reject Jerusalem and the temple. The structural parallel with the northern kingdom’s fate (2 Kings 17:18–20) is complete: what was done to Ephraim will now be done to Judah. The rejection of Jerusalem and the ‘chosen’ temple contradicts the conditional promise of 1 Kings 9:3; the condition of covenant faithfulness was never consistently met.
28: The standard regnal closing formula for Josiah refers the reader to the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Judah. This administrative archive — distinct from the canonical book of Chronicles — was presumably maintained by royal scribes and contained records of military campaigns, building projects, and fiscal administration. The formula implies that Josiah’s full political and military record — including his northern campaigns — existed in accessible archives.
30: Josiah’s servants bring his body from Megiddo to Jerusalem in a chariot and bury him in his own tomb. The ceremonial transfer of the royal body — wayyarkịḇûhû mēt mimmegiddô (they carried him dead from Megiddo) — reflects the ANE protocol for royal burial. The people anoint Jehoahaz as king; the use of anointing (wayyimšᵉḥû) rather than mere accession is unusual and may indicate disputed succession: Jehoahaz was not the eldest son.
31: Jehoahaz is twenty-three years old at accession, reigning only three months (609 BCE). His brief reign is the shortest of any Judahite king. The regnal formula places his mother as Hamutal from Libnah — a Judahite town — indicating his maternal lineage differed from his half-brother Eliakim (Jehoiakim), whose mother was from Rumah. The different mothers may reflect different court factions supporting different succession candidates.
32: Jehoahaz does evil in YHWH’s sight according to all his ancestors did — the standard negative formula indicating covenant violation, though without specifics. Given the brevity of his reign, the evaluation is likely deuteronomistic shorthand rather than reflecting detailed historical knowledge of his three-month rule. He was presumably not hostile to Josiah’s reforms given his selection by the people over his older brother.
33: Pharaoh Neco imprisons Jehoahaz at Riblah in the land of Hamath and imposes a tribute of one hundred talents of silver and one talent of gold on Judah. Riblah on the Orontes in central Syria served as the main Egyptian military headquarters in the Levant during 609–605 BCE — the same site where Nebuchadnezzar later operated (2 Kings 25:6). The tribute amounts are heavy: one talent of gold = approximately 30 kg; 100 talents of silver = approximately 3,000 kg.
34: Pharaoh Neco makes Eliakim son of Josiah king and changes his name to Jehoiakim; Jehoahaz is taken to Egypt where he dies. The name change from Eliakim (‘God establishes’) to Jehoiakim (‘YHWH establishes’) is paradoxical — Neco, a foreign king, chooses a more explicitly YHWH-centered throne name for his vassal. In the ANE, a suzerain’s renaming of a vassal king expressed dominion over the vassal’s identity. Jehoahaz died in Egypt (Jeremiah 22:11–12).
35: Jehoiakim taxes the land to pay Pharaoh Neco — exacting silver and gold from the people of the land proportionally. The pro-rated taxation (ʿal pî ʿarāḵô, ‘according to his assessment’) reflects a property-based taxation system. The mechanism — Jehoiakim collects from landholders and pays Pharaoh — shows Judah functioning as an Egyptian revenue stream. Jeremiah’s oracle against Jehoiakim (Jeremiah 22:13–19) criticizes his use of forced labor for his own building projects during this period.
36: Jehoiakim is twenty-five years old at accession, reigning eleven years in Jerusalem (609–598 BCE). His mother Zebidah from Rumah has an unusual name — the name is unique in the OT. Jehoiakim’s eleven-year reign spans the critical transition from Egyptian to Babylonian hegemony in the Levant, culminating in his rebellion against Nebuchadnezzar that preceded the siege of 597 BCE.
37: Jehoiakim does evil in YHWH’s sight according to all his ancestors. The evaluation without specification stands in contrast to the extensive prophetic record of Jehoiakim’s specific crimes in Jeremiah — burning Jeremiah’s scroll (Jeremiah 36:23–25), executing the prophet Uriah (Jeremiah 26:20–23), building an extravagant palace with forced labor (Jeremiah 22:13–17). Kings’ silence on these details reflects its genre’s different focus from the prophetic books.
